agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics platform operating in EU and UK markets proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform serves B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a fundamental business and regulatory compliance change with potentially catastrophic consequences.","bounded_context":"Delivery Records / Data Lifecycle Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete dataset associated with a completed delivery: tracking events, proof of delivery, recipient signature, timestamps, address data, parcel details, and associated financial transactions."},{"term":"Data Retention Policy","definition":"The rules governing how long delivery records are stored, driven by legal obligations, contractual commitments, and business needs — not storage cost optimization."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a delivery was completed — signatures, photos, GPS coordinates, timestamps. Required for dispute resolution and contractual compliance."},{"term":"B2B Compliance Reporting","definition":"The use of delivery data by business customers for their own regulatory, tax, audit, and contractual compliance obligations."},{"term":"Right to Erasure","definition":"GDPR Article 17 right allowing data subjects to request deletion, subject to legal retention obligations that override this right."},{"term":"Statutory Retention Period","definition":"The legally mandated minimum period for which certain records must be kept, which varies by jurisdiction and record type."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records that serve as evidence for tax, customs, or VAT transactions MUST be retained for the statutory minimum period required by applicable EU/UK tax law (typically 6-7 years).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Violating statutory retention requirements exposes the company and its B2B customers to regulatory penalties, failed audits, and legal liability. This is a legal constraint, not a policy choice.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record linked to a taxable transaction is deleted before the statutory retention period has elapsed."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery records required by B2B customers for their own compliance reporting MUST be retained for the duration specified in contractual SLAs with those customers.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Deleting data that B2B customers contractually depend on for their compliance constitutes a breach of contract and could expose those customers to regulatory penalties.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record subject to a B2B contractual retention SLA is deleted before that SLA period expires."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Proof of Delivery records must be available for the entire consumer dispute resolution window, including chargeback and legal claim limitation periods (typically 2-6 years depending on jurisdiction).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without POD data, the platform and its customers cannot defend against chargebacks, fraud claims, or consumer disputes. Deletion within 90 days would destroy evidence needed for active and future disputes.","testable_assertion":"POD records are retrievable for at least the full limitation period for contractual claims in the applicable jurisdiction."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Any change to data retention policy must undergo a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) as required by GDPR Article 35 before implementation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"GDPR mandates DPIAs for processing changes that affect personal data at scale. Skipping this step is itself a regulatory violation.","testable_assertion":"A completed DPIA exists and is approved before any retention policy change is deployed to production."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Data retention periods must be segmented by data category — personal identifiable information, financial/tax records, and operational metadata may have different retention requirements.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a best-practice architectural approach rather than a physical constraint, but it reflects the reality that a single blanket retention period cannot satisfy all legal obligations.","testable_assertion":"The retention policy defines distinct periods for at least: PII, financial/tax records, and operational/analytics data."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A delivery record is linked to a VAT or customs transaction in an EU/UK jurisdiction","when":"The auto-deletion process evaluates it for deletion","then":"The record is NOT deleted until the full statutory tax retention period (typically 6-7 years) has elapsed"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A B2B customer has an active contract with a data retention SLA","when":"The auto-deletion process evaluates their delivery records","then":"No records are deleted before the contractually agreed retention period expires"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A delivery has associated Proof of Delivery data","when":"The auto-deletion process evaluates it within the consumer dispute limitation period","then":"The POD and related records are retained for the full limitation period"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"The engineering team proposes a retention policy change","when":"The change is submitted for review","then":"A completed DPIA is required before the change can be approved for production deployment"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A data subject exercises their Right to Erasure under GDPR","when":"The platform processes the erasure request","then":"Records subject to statutory or contractual retention obligations are exempt from erasure, and the data subject is informed of the legal basis for continued retention"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A B2B customer requests delivery data for their compliance audit","when":"The data falls within the contractually agreed retention window","then":"The full delivery record is available and retrievable"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Cross-border delivery spanning multiple EU jurisdictions with different tax retention requirements","expected_behavior":"The longest applicable statutory retention period across all involved jurisdictions governs the record's retention."},{"scenario":"B2B customer contract expires but statutory retention period has not elapsed","expected_behavior":"Records are retained for the statutory minimum regardless of contract status. Only the contractual access SLA changes, not the retention obligation."},{"scenario":"Active legal dispute or litigation hold on delivery records approaching 90-day mark","expected_behavior":"Records under litigation hold are NEVER auto-deleted regardless of any retention policy. Legal holds override all automated deletion."},{"scenario":"Delivery record contains personal data of both sender and recipient across different jurisdictions","expected_behavior":"Retention is governed by the most restrictive applicable requirement across all data subjects and jurisdictions involved."},{"scenario":"B2B customer goes bankrupt but their historical delivery data is needed by a tax authority investigation","expected_behavior":"Statutory retention obligations to tax authorities survive customer bankruptcy. Data must remain available for the statutory period."},{"scenario":"90-day deletion runs but record has pending chargeback from payment processor","expected_behavior":"Records linked to open financial disputes are never deleted. Deletion is blocked until dispute resolution is complete AND the retention period has elapsed."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE. Auto-deleting tax-relevant delivery records exposes the company to regulatory fines (GDPR fines up to 4% of global annual turnover, plus separate tax authority penalties). B2B customers losing compliance data could trigger breach-of-contract claims and churn of high-value accounts. Chargeback disputes without POD evidence become automatic losses.","operational":"CRITICAL. This change requires a complete reclassification of delivery records by category, legal basis for retention, and jurisdiction. A blanket 90-day policy is operationally simpler but legally non-viable. The correct approach requires a tiered retention architecture — significantly MORE complex than the current 7-year blanket policy, not less.","user":"HIGH. B2B customers who rely on historical delivery data for compliance reporting would lose access to critical records. B2C consumers would lose the ability to raise disputes or obtain proof of delivery beyond 90 days. Trust in the platform as a reliable system of record would be severely damaged.","risk":"EXTREME. This change as proposed would almost certainly violate: (1) EU/UK tax record retention laws, (2) GDPR requirements for lawful processing and DPIAs, (3) contractual SLAs with B2B customers, (4) consumer protection dispute resolution requirements. The framing as 'no business logic changes' is factually incorrect and dangerous — it could bypass the review processes that would catch these violations.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"Loss of delivery data needed for their own tax, customs, and regulatory compliance. Could trigger cascading compliance failures across their businesses and contractual breach claims against the platform."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"Loss of proof of delivery, inability to raise disputes or chargebacks beyond 90 days, erosion of consumer protection rights."},{"group":"Finance / Tax Department","impact":"Inability to produce records required for VAT audits, customs inspections, or tax authority inquiries. Direct exposure to penalties."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance Team","impact":"Loss of evidence for defending disputes, chargebacks, and litigation. GDPR DPIA requirement likely not being met if this is treated as pure infrastructure."},{"group":"Engineering Team","impact":"If implemented as proposed and later found non-compliant, an emergency data recovery effort may be impossible — deleted data is unrecoverable."},{"group":"Company Leadership / Board","impact":"Regulatory fines, reputational damage, potential personal liability for directors under GDPR accountability obligations."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The spec quality is extremely poor. A change with massive regulatory, contractual, and business implications has been framed as a simple infrastructure optimization with 'no business logic changes.' Critical information — legal review, DPIA, contractual SLA analysis, jurisdiction-specific retention requirements — has not been provided by anyone. Without input from Legal, Compliance, Tax, and affected B2B customers, the true minimum retention requirements per data category and jurisdiction are unknown.","high_confidence_areas":["EU/UK tax retention laws generally require 6-7 years for transaction-related records","GDPR requires a DPIA for significant changes to personal data processing","B2B customers using delivery data for compliance will be directly impacted","90 days is insufficient for consumer dispute and chargeback windows","The framing as 'no business logic changes' is incorrect"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact statutory retention periods per EU member state and UK post-Brexit — need Legal input","Specific contractual SLAs with B2B customers — need commercial/contracts team input","Whether any delivery records genuinely have no legal retention requirement and could safely be deleted at 90 days","Platform's current GDPR Article 30 records of processing activities — need DPO input"],"assumptions":["The platform processes deliveries that involve taxable transactions subject to VAT","B2B customer contracts include data availability or retention SLAs","The platform stores personal data as part of delivery records","No DPIA has been conducted for this proposed change"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"No legal or regulatory review has been provided for this retention policy change. EU/UK tax law, GDPR retention obligations, consumer protection limitation periods, and jurisdiction-specific requirements have not been analyzed by qualified legal counsel. This information was never provided by anyone in the request.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance Team","B2B Customers","Finance/Tax Department","Company Leadership"],"options":["Halt the proposal until Legal and DPO complete a full regulatory retention analysis and DPIA","Proceed with the 90-day deletion for non-regulated operational metadata only, after legal categorization of data types","Maintain 7-year retention as-is and optimize storage costs through compression, tiering, or cold storage instead"],"recommendation":"REJECT the proposal as stated. Mandate a legal/regulatory review and DPIA before any retention policy change is considered. In parallel, explore storage cost reduction through tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) rather than deletion — this achieves the cost optimization goal without regulatory risk."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"B2B customer contractual SLAs regarding data retention and availability have not been reviewed. Deleting data that customers contractually depend on is a breach of contract.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Logistics Customers","Commercial/Sales Team","Legal Team"],"options":["Review all active B2B contracts for data retention clauses before proceeding","Notify B2B customers and negotiate amended terms","Maintain current retention for B2B customer data regardless of infrastructure changes"],"recommendation":"Commercial and Legal teams must audit all B2B contracts for data retention obligations before any policy change."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"The Data Protection Officer (DPO) has not been consulted, and no DPIA has been conducted. Under GDPR Article 35, a DPIA is mandatory before implementing changes to data processing that could result in high risk to data subjects' rights.","affected_stakeholders":["DPO","All data subjects (senders, recipients, B2B users)","Company Leadership"],"options":["Conduct a DPIA before proceeding","Seek guidance from the relevant supervisory authority","Abandon the blanket deletion approach"],"recommendation":"DPIA must be completed and approved before any implementation work begins. This is not optional under GDPR."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Proposal framed as 'no business logic changes' when it fundamentally alters data availability for tax compliance, dispute resolution, and B2B contractual obligations.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2, BR-3"},{"violation":"No Data Protection Impact Assessment conducted for a change that would delete personal data of EU/UK data subjects at massive scale.","business_rule_broken":"BR-4"},{"violation":"Blanket 90-day retention applied uniformly to all data categories, ignoring that different record types have fundamentally different legal retention requirements.","business_rule_broken":"BR-5"},{"violation":"Auto-deletion of delivery records within 90 days would destroy Proof of Delivery evidence needed for consumer disputes and chargebacks that can arise years after delivery.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics platform operating in EU and UK markets proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform serves B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a fundamental business and regulatory compliance change with potentially catastrophic consequences.","bounded_context":"Delivery

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this carefully. This is a data retention policy change for delivery records in a logistics platform operating in EU and UK markets. The engineering team frames it as "infrastructure optimization" but this has massive domain implications.

Key concerns:
1. GDPR requires certain data retention but also limits it
2. UK GDPR post-Brexit
3. Tax/customs records retention requirements (typically 6-7 years in EU)
4. B2B customers depend on this data for compliance reporting
5. Consumer dispute resolution windows
6. The framing as "no business logic changes" is dangerously wrong

This is clearly a missing_context situation - nobody has provided legal/regulatory review, and the regulatory retention requirements are critical but never stated by anyone in the scenario.


Now I'm working through the actual analysis of what needs to be flagged here.
